author: @sykoteddy
text: Thank you for clearifying this! I've been scrapping in private for some 8 years but on such a small scale I don't have enough of gold on the PCBs yet to try anything out. Just to be able to use the bizarre title.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text: If you can tune the way the fibers align, and the structures of the original material used to make charcoal effect the way the charcoal reacts when activated (if I remember correctly from a previous video) could you tune this to the desired alignment and then burn it to create charcoal, would it retain those properties?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes as long as you did the carbonisation using hydrothermal carbonisation which i have done videos on how to do

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: I did this just to say I did it. I used the hydrogen peroxide method, then bleached it, adding stump-out as my precipitate. Out of 120+ computers I got a quarter ounce. And I still scrapped everything else. Cases and power supplies sold as scrap. I still have several hundred of hard drives, which equate to 2 pounds of aluminum each. Lesson learned; you will never get your time back from the process. But you can feel good about the scrapping process. Would I do it again? There isn't an available source for that many free machines any longer.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
Nice.  And good to be back on to casein -- I find those earlier videos of yours fascinating.
Of course, if the number of cattle is reduced because meat is somehow dangerous to the environment, the price of milk will go up as production rate goes down.  
It would be nice to recover the zinc and copper from circuit boards, as well as the gold. Perhaps electrolytically? 
 If the lead was also recovered, then some amount of heavy metal pollution would be prevented.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: IRC after removing copper and zinc the lead is just a matter of evaporation deposits.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
But copper, lead and tin are all valuable metals people SHOULD be harvesting and recycling because THIS KEEPS those from ending up as soluble waste in the environment!

As I have worked with the waste disposal industry, I have learned that BY FAR, MOST of the toxic waste that gets into the environment GETS THERE via common landfill leachate, which, no matter how much they strive to prevent that, is simply impossible.

So if you, the individual, take the extra time to recycle that material, you can not only make more money, but also protect the environment FAR more effectively!

author: @Ammoniummetavanadate
text: I can smell that floatation cell just looking at it. Potassium Xanthate, if you know you know.

author: @robertsmith4681
text: One thing I have often wondered about is gold recovery from seawater. This technique sounds a lot like applied bioaccumulation which at one point showed promise for things like soil decomtamnation

replies.author: @nonsequitor
replies.text: The soil decontamination route is still a thing , it's just that as usual, profit margin rules ok. Re: seawater: interesting, but you'd get way better yield starting out with beach sand, soil etc although granted, sea water is a lot easier to handle 👌

replies.author: @robertsmith4681
replies.text: @nonsequitor I'm thinking of something along the lines of using a solar still to boil seawater into brine somewhat passively and then filter out solids and heavy metals, process the dirty filters as if they were ore basically. Assuming the filters let thru some of the lighter metals the "filtered" brine would likely end up with useful amounts of things like lithium. I genuinely wonder how much metal is in something like a cubic meter of sea salt, and how much of that is gold, copper, silver, lead and so on.

replies.author: @jhehn3
